source.definition: To expel air through the mouth or to fill something with air by forcing air into it

explanation.sourceText: The English word "blow" originates from Old English "blāwan", meaning to blow or breathe out air. It is related to similar words in other Germanic languages and has been used since early medieval times.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „blow” provine din engleza veche „blāwan”, care înseamnă a sufla sau a expira aer. Este înrudit cu cuvinte similare din alte limbi germanice și este folosit încă din perioada medievală timpurie.

source.additional: Regular verb, commonly used in contexts involving air or gases being forced out. Frequently appears in compound forms and idiomatic expressions related to air movement.

target.definition: a împinge aer prin ceva sau a umple ceva cu aer

explanation.sourceText: The verb „a sufla“ derives from the Latin „sufflare“, meaning to blow. It is composed of „sub-“ (under) and „flare“ (to blow), describing the action of expelling air.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a sufla“ provine din latină, din „sufflare“, format din „sub-“ (sub) și „flare“ (a sufla), însemnând a expulza aer.

target.additional: Regular first conjugation verb. Important in everyday language use, especially when playing instruments or lighting fires.

explanation.sourceText: Air is used to create a sound, for example when playing an instrument.
explanation.targetText: Aerul este folosit pentru a produce sunete, de exemplu la un instrument muzical.

explanation.sourceText: To blow air through something in order to inflate it.
explanation.targetText: A sufla aer prin ceva pentru a-l umfla.

examples.sourceText: Can you please blow the dust away?
Are you blowing at the party?
I have blown up the balloon.
He blew air into the balloon.
I would blow the melody if I could.
examples.targetText: Aș sufla melodia, dacă aș putea.

learningTips.text: Remember the Romanian verb "a sufla" sounds like "soof-lah" — think of softly blowing air through a flute to produce music.
Use "a sufla" whenever you talk about blowing out candles, inflating balloons, or blowing on a fire to keep it alive.
Picture yourself blowing air into a balloon — this image helps connect the action with the word "a sufla".
The root "suf-" in "a sufla" is similar to the English word "suffocate" which also relates to air and breathing, helping you remember the meaning.
